This section walks through one complete iteration of XGBoost with real numbers. Every gradient, every Hessian, every Gain calculation, and every weight update is computed explicitly. By the end, you will have seen exactly what happens inside the algorithm on a single step.

🔹 GIVEN DATA

Point True Value (yiy_iyi​) Current Prediction (y^i\hat{y}_iy^​i​)
1 100 80
2 200 220
3 500 450

What we see: The model is currently underpredicting Point 1 by 20, overpredicting Point 2 by 20, and underpredicting Point 3 by 50.

🔹 STEP 1: DEFINE THE LOSS FUNCTION

We use Mean Squared Error (MSE):

l(y,y^)=(y−y^)2l(y, \hat{y}) = (y - \hat{y})^2l(y,y^​)=(y−y^​)2

Why MSE?

  • Differentiable everywhere
  • Penalizes large errors strongly
  • Simple gradient and Hessian

🔹 STEP 2: COMPUTE GRADIENT & HESSIAN

📌 FORMULAS FOR MSE

Loss:

l(yi,y^i)=(yi−y^i)2l(y_i, \hat{y}_i) = (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2l(yi​,y^​i​)=(yi​−y^​i​)2

Gradient (first derivative):

gi=∂l∂y^i=∂∂y^i(yi−y^i)2g_i = \frac{\partial l}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}_i}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2gi​=∂y^​i​∂l​=∂y^​i​∂​(yi​−y^​i​)2

Using chain rule:

  • Let u=yi−y^iu = y_i - \hat{y}_iu=yi​−y^​i​
  • l=u2l = u^2l=u2
  • ∂l∂u=2u\frac{\partial l}{\partial u} = 2u∂u∂l​=2u
  • ∂u∂y^i=−1\frac{\partial u}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = -1∂y^​i​∂u​=−1
gi=2(yi−y^i)⋅(−1)=−2(yi−y^i)=2(y^i−yi)g_i = 2(y_i - \hat{y}_i) \cdot (-1) = -2(y_i - \hat{y}_i) = 2(\hat{y}_i - y_i)gi​=2(yi​−y^​i​)⋅(−1)=−2(yi​−y^​i​)=2(y^​i​−yi​)

Hessian (second derivative):

hi=∂gi∂y^i=∂∂y^i[−2(yi−y^i)]=−2⋅(−1)=2h_i = \frac{\partial g_i}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}_i}[-2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)] = -2 \cdot (-1) = 2hi​=∂y^​i​∂gi​​=∂y^​i​∂​[−2(yi​−y^​i​)]=−2⋅(−1)=2

Key insight: For MSE, the Hessian is constant (hi=2h_i = 2hi​=2) for all samples. This makes MSE the simplest case.

📊 COMPUTE VALUES FOR EACH POINT

Point yiy_iyi​ y^i\hat{y}_iy^​i​ Error yi−y^iy_i - \hat{y}_iyi​−y^​i​ gi=−2(yi−y^i)g_i = -2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)gi​=−2(yi​−y^​i​) hih_ihi​
1 100 80 20 −2(20)=−40-2(20) = -40−2(20)=−40 2
2 200 220 -20 −2(−20)=+40-2(-20) = +40−2(−20)=+40 2
3 500 450 50 −2(50)=−100-2(50) = -100−2(50)=−100 2

Interpretation of gradients:

  • g1=−40g_1 = -40g1​=−40: Prediction is too LOW. We need to INCREASE it.
  • g2=+40g_2 = +40g2​=+40: Prediction is too HIGH. We need to DECREASE it.
  • g3=−100g_3 = -100g3​=−100: Prediction is too LOW (by a lot). We need to INCREASE it significantly.

The negative gradient −gi-g_i−gi​ tells us which direction to move:

  • Point 1: move +40+40+40
  • Point 2: move −40-40−40
  • Point 3: move +100+100+100

🔹 STEP 3: ROOT NODE (BEFORE ANY SPLIT)

Before splitting, all 3 points are in one node (the root).

Aggregate statistics:

G=∑i=13gi=(−40)+40+(−100)=−100G = \sum_{i=1}^{3} g_i = (-40) + 40 + (-100) = -100G=i=1∑3​gi​=(−40)+40+(−100)=−100
H=∑i=13hi=2+2+2=6H = \sum_{i=1}^{3} h_i = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6H=i=1∑3​hi​=2+2+2=6

Root Score (quality of this leaf):

Scoreroot=G2H+λ=(−100)26+0=100006≈1666.67\text{Score}_{\text{root}} = \frac{G^2}{H + \lambda} = \frac{(-100)^2}{6 + 0} = \frac{10000}{6} \approx 1666.67Scoreroot​=H+λG2​=6+0(−100)2​=610000​≈1666.67

What this means: This score measures how much this leaf reduces the objective. Higher = better. We use it to compare before/after splits.

🔹 STEP 4: TRY A SPLIT

We try splitting the data. Let’s test this split:

Split rule: Separate Point 3 from Points 1 and 2.

Side Points Members
Left {1, 2} Point 1 and Point 2
Right {3} Point 3

📊 LEFT NODE (Points 1 and 2)

GL=g1+g2=(−40)+40=0G_L = g_1 + g_2 = (-40) + 40 = 0GL​=g1​+g2​=(−40)+40=0
HL=h1+h2=2+2=4H_L = h_1 + h_2 = 2 + 2 = 4HL​=h1​+h2​=2+2=4

Left Score:

ScoreL=GL2HL+λ=024+0=0\text{Score}_L = \frac{G_L^2}{H_L + \lambda} = \frac{0^2}{4 + 0} = 0ScoreL​=HL​+λGL2​​=4+002​=0

Interpretation: The left node has zero net gradient. Points 1 and 2 have opposite errors that cancel out. This node is already “balanced” — no strong correction needed.

📊 RIGHT NODE (Point 3)

GR=g3=−100G_R = g_3 = -100GR​=g3​=−100
HR=h3=2H_R = h_3 = 2HR​=h3​=2

Right Score:

ScoreR=GR2HR+λ=(−100)22+0=100002=5000\text{Score}_R = \frac{G_R^2}{H_R + \lambda} = \frac{(-100)^2}{2 + 0} = \frac{10000}{2} = 5000ScoreR​=HR​+λGR2​​=2+0(−100)2​=210000​=5000

Interpretation: The right node has a strong negative gradient. This node needs a large positive correction.

🔹 STEP 5: COMPUTE GAIN

The Gain formula:

Gain=12[ScoreL+ScoreR−Scoreroot]−γ\text{Gain} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\text{Score}_L + \text{Score}_R - \text{Score}_{\text{root}}\right] - \gammaGain=21​[ScoreL​+ScoreR​−Scoreroot​]−γ

With λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0 and γ=0\gamma = 0γ=0:

Gain=12[0+5000−1666.67]−0\text{Gain} = \frac{1}{2}\left[0 + 5000 - 1666.67\right] - 0Gain=21​[0+5000−1666.67]−0
Gain=12[3333.33]=1666.67\text{Gain} = \frac{1}{2}\left[3333.33\right] = 1666.67Gain=21​[3333.33]=1666.67

🧠 DECISION

Condition Value Action
Gain > 0 1666.67 > 0 ✅ ACCEPT SPLIT

Why accept? The split improves the objective by 1666.67 units. Since this exceeds γ=0\gamma = 0γ=0, we accept.

🔹 STEP 6: COMPUTE LEAF WEIGHTS

The optimal leaf weight formula:

wj∗=−GjHj+λw_j^* = -\frac{G_j}{H_j + \lambda}wj∗​=−Hj​+λGj​​

With λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0:

📊 LEFT LEAF (Points 1 and 2)

wL∗=−GLHL=−04=0w_L^* = -\frac{G_L}{H_L} = -\frac{0}{4} = 0wL∗​=−HL​GL​​=−40​=0

Interpretation: No correction needed. Points 1 and 2 have opposite errors that cancel out.

📊 RIGHT LEAF (Point 3)

wR∗=−GRHR=−−1002=50w_R^* = -\frac{G_R}{H_R} = -\frac{-100}{2} = 50wR∗​=−HR​GR​​=−2−100​=50

Interpretation: Add +50 to the prediction for Point 3. This corrects the large underprediction.

🔹 STEP 7: UPDATE PREDICTIONS

Update rule with shrinkage:

y^inew=y^i+η⋅wj∗\hat{y}_i^{\text{new}} = \hat{y}_i + \eta \cdot w_j^*y^​inew​=y^​i​+η⋅wj∗​

Where:

  • η=0.3\eta = 0.3η=0.3 (learning rate)
  • wj∗w_j^*wj∗​ is the leaf weight for the leaf that point iii falls into

📊 POINT-BY-POINT UPDATE

Point Old y^\hat{y}y^​ Leaf w∗w^*w∗ η⋅w∗\eta \cdot w^*η⋅w∗ New y^\hat{y}y^​
1 80 Left 0 0.3×0=00.3 \times 0 = 00.3×0=0 80+0=8080 + 0 = 8080+0=80
2 220 Left 0 0.3×0=00.3 \times 0 = 00.3×0=0 220+0=220220 + 0 = 220220+0=220
3 450 Right 50 0.3×50=150.3 \times 50 = 150.3×50=15 450+15=465450 + 15 = 465450+15=465

📊 RESULTS AFTER ONE ITERATION

Point True yyy Old y^\hat{y}y^​ New y^\hat{y}y^​ New Error
1 100 80 80 20
2 200 220 220 -20
3 500 450 465 -35

What changed:

  • Points 1 and 2: Unchanged (their errors cancel out, no correction needed)
  • Point 3: Improved from error of 50 to error of 35

Why only Point 3 changed?

  • The split isolated Point 3 because it had the strongest gradient signal
  • The tree learned: “Point 3 is different from Points 1 and 2”
  • Points 1 and 2 will be handled in future iterations

🧠 FINAL INTERPRETATION

🔥 WHAT HAPPENED IN THIS ONE ITERATION

Step What Happened Why
1 Computed gradients Found that Point 3 has the largest error
2 Built tree with one split Isolated Point 3 from the others
3 Computed leaf weights Left leaf = 0 (balanced), Right leaf = 50 (needs +50)
4 Applied shrinkage (η=0.3\eta = 0.3η=0.3) Added only 15 instead of full 50
5 Updated predictions Point 3 improved, others stayed same

📊 COMPARISON: WITH AND WITHOUT SHRINKAGE

Scenario Point 3 New Prediction Assessment
Without shrinkage (η=1\eta = 1η=1) 450+50=500450 + 50 = 500450+50=500 Exact match! But too aggressive.
With shrinkage (η=0.3\eta = 0.3η=0.3) 450+15=465450 + 15 = 465450+15=465 Partial correction. Safer.

Why shrinkage is better: If we jumped to 500 immediately, we might overshoot if the true pattern is more complex. With η=0.3\eta = 0.3η=0.3, we take a small step and let future trees refine.

⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES (IMPORTANT)

❌ Wrong ✅ Correct Why
gi=2(yi−y^i)g_i = 2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)gi​=2(yi​−y^​i​) gi=−2(yi−y^i)g_i = -2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)gi​=−2(yi​−y^​i​) Sign matters! Gradient is w.r.t prediction, not true value
Ignore Hessian Always include hi=2h_i = 2hi​=2 Required for correct leaf weight
Skip learning rate Final update must include η\etaη Shrinkage prevents overfitting
w∗=G/Hw^* = G/Hw∗=G/H w∗=−G/Hw^* = -G/Hw∗=−G/H Negative sign is critical
Update all points equally Only update points in the leaf Each leaf has different weight

🔬 VERIFICATION: CHECK THE OBJECTIVE IMPROVEMENT

Before split (root only):

Objectivebefore=∑i=13gi⋅0+12⋅6⋅02=0\text{Objective}_{\text{before}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} g_i \cdot 0 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 0^2 = 0Objectivebefore​=i=1∑3​gi​⋅0+21​⋅6⋅02=0

(Using w=0w = 0w=0 as initial guess for root leaf)

Actually, let’s compute properly using the optimal root weight:

wroot∗=−GH=−−1006=16.67w_{\text{root}}^* = -\frac{G}{H} = -\frac{-100}{6} = 16.67wroot∗​=−HG​=−6−100​=16.67
Objectivebefore=G⋅wroot∗+12H(wroot∗)2\text{Objective}_{\text{before}} = G \cdot w_{\text{root}}^* + \frac{1}{2} H (w_{\text{root}}^*)^2Objectivebefore​=G⋅wroot∗​+21​H(wroot∗​)2
=(−100)(16.67)+12(6)(16.67)2= (-100)(16.67) + \frac{1}{2}(6)(16.67)^2=(−100)(16.67)+21​(6)(16.67)2
=−1667+833.33=−833.33= -1667 + 833.33 = -833.33=−1667+833.33=−833.33

After split (two leaves):

Left: wL∗=0w_L^* = 0wL∗​=0, Right: wR∗=50w_R^* = 50wR∗​=50

Objectiveafter=[GL⋅0+12HL⋅02]+[GR⋅50+12HR⋅502]\text{Objective}_{\text{after}} = \left[G_L \cdot 0 + \frac{1}{2}H_L \cdot 0^2\right] + \left[G_R \cdot 50 + \frac{1}{2}H_R \cdot 50^2\right]Objectiveafter​=[GL​⋅0+21​HL​⋅02]+[GR​⋅50+21​HR​⋅502]
=0+[(−100)(50)+12(2)(2500)]= 0 + \left[(-100)(50) + \frac{1}{2}(2)(2500)\right]=0+[(−100)(50)+21​(2)(2500)]
=−5000+2500=−2500= -5000 + 2500 = -2500=−5000+2500=−2500

Improvement:

Δ=Objectiveafter−Objectivebefore=−2500−(−833.33)=−1666.67\Delta = \text{Objective}_{\text{after}} - \text{Objective}_{\text{before}} = -2500 - (-833.33) = -1666.67Δ=Objectiveafter​−Objectivebefore​=−2500−(−833.33)=−1666.67

The objective decreased by 1666.67, which matches our Gain calculation!

Verification successful. The math is consistent.

🧾 FINAL CHEAT SHEET (ULTRA-CLEAN)

Write this in your notes:

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║     XGBOOST: ONE COMPLETE ITERATION — NUMERICAL EXAMPLE              ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                      ║
║  DATA:                                                               ║
║    Point 1: y=100, ŷ=80                                              ║
║    Point 2: y=200, ŷ=220                                             ║
║    Point 3: y=500, ŷ=450                                             ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  STEP 1: LOSS = MSE                                                  ║
║    l(y, ŷ) = (y - ŷ)²                                               ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  STEP 2: GRADIENT & HESSIAN                                          ║
║    gᵢ = -2(yᵢ - ŷᵢ) = 2(ŷᵢ - yᵢ)                                    ║
║    hᵢ = 2 (constant for MSE)                                         ║
║                                                                      ║
║    Point 1: g₁ = -40, h₁ = 2                                         ║
║    Point 2: g₂ = +40, h₂ = 2                                         ║
║    Point 3: g₃ = -100, h₃ = 2                                        ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  STEP 3: ROOT NODE                                                   ║
║    G = -100, H = 6                                                   ║
║    Score_root = G²/H = 10000/6 = 1666.67                            ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  STEP 4: TRY SPLIT {1,2} | {3}                                       ║
║    Left:  G_L = 0,   H_L = 4,   Score_L = 0                         ║
║    Right: G_R = -100, H_R = 2,   Score_R = 5000                      ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  STEP 5: GAIN                                                        ║
║    Gain = ½(Score_L + Score_R - Score_root) - γ                     ║
║          = ½(0 + 5000 - 1666.67) = 1666.67                            ║
║    Gain > 0 → ✅ ACCEPT SPLIT                                        ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  STEP 6: LEAF WEIGHTS                                                ║
║    w_L* = -G_L/H_L = -0/4 = 0                                        ║
║    w_R* = -G_R/H_R = -(-100)/2 = 50                                  ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  STEP 7: UPDATE (η = 0.3)                                            ║
║    Point 1: ŷ_new = 80 + 0.3(0) = 80                                 ║
║    Point 2: ŷ_new = 220 + 0.3(0) = 220                               ║
║    Point 3: ŷ_new = 450 + 0.3(50) = 465                              ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════  ║
║  RESULT:                                                             ║
║    • Point 3 error reduced: 50 → 35                                  ║
║    • Points 1,2 unchanged (opposite errors cancel)                   ║
║    • Shrinkage prevents overshoot                                    ║
║                                                                      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

📝 SPACE FOR YOUR HANDWRITTEN NOTES

[Draw the tree created in this iteration: Root splits into Left (Points 1,2, weight=0) and Right (Point 3, weight=50). Label each node with G, H, and Score. Show the prediction update arrows: Point 3 gets +15, others get +0.]

[Draw a table showing the before/after predictions with color coding: green for improved, yellow for unchanged.]

[Write the gradient sign convention prominently: “g = -2(y - ŷ)” and “Negative gradient = direction to move”.]

🧪 QUICK SELF-CHECK

Q1: In the example, why does Point 2 have a positive gradient (+40) while Points 1 and 3 have negative gradients?

Answer: Point 2 is overpredicted (y^=220>y=200\hat{y} = 220 > y = 200y^​=220>y=200). The gradient g=−2(y−y^)=−2(−20)=+40g = -2(y - \hat{y}) = -2(-20) = +40g=−2(y−y^​)=−2(−20)=+40 is positive because we need to decrease the prediction. The sign tells us the direction: negative gradient means “increase prediction,” positive gradient means “decrease prediction.”

Q2: What would happen if we set λ=10\lambda = 10λ=10 instead of λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0 in this example?

Answer:

  • Root: wroot∗=−(−100)/(6+10)=100/16=6.25w_{\text{root}}^* = -(-100)/(6+10) = 100/16 = 6.25wroot∗​=−(−100)/(6+10)=100/16=6.25 (instead of 16.67)
  • Right leaf: wR∗=−(−100)/(2+10)=100/12=8.33w_R^* = -(-100)/(2+10) = 100/12 = 8.33wR∗​=−(−100)/(2+10)=100/12=8.33 (instead of 50)
  • Update for Point 3: 450+0.3×8.33=452.5450 + 0.3 \times 8.33 = 452.5450+0.3×8.33=452.5 (instead of 465)

The correction is much smaller. Strong regularization makes the model conservative.

Q3: Why is the left leaf weight zero even though Point 1 has error?

Answer: The left leaf contains Points 1 and 2 with gradients -40 and +40. They cancel out: GL=0G_L = 0GL​=0. The net error in this leaf is zero, so no correction is needed at this level. The algorithm will handle Point 1 in a future iteration with a different split.

Q4: If η=1.0\eta = 1.0η=1.0, what would be the new prediction for Point 3? Why is this dangerous?

Answer: y^3new=450+1.0×50=500\hat{y}_3^{\text{new}} = 450 + 1.0 \times 50 = 500y^​3new​=450+1.0×50=500. This exactly matches the true value! But it’s dangerous because:

  • We might have overfit to this single point
  • The “true” pattern might not be exactly +50 for this region
  • Future trees can’t correct an overshoot (trees are never modified)

Q5: Verify that the Gain we computed (1666.67) equals the actual objective improvement.

Answer: See the “Verification” section above. The objective before = -833.33, after = -2500. Improvement = -2500 - (-833.33) = -1666.67. The magnitude matches Gain = 1666.67. ✓

🔥 FINAL DEEP INSIGHT

XGBoost is a system that combines:

  1. Second-order optimization (Newton-like leaf weights)
  2. Greedy tree search (maximize Gain at each split)
  3. Multi-level regularization (η\etaη, γ\gammaγ, λ\lambdaλ)
  4. Hardware-aware execution (column blocks, cache prefetching)

In this one iteration, we saw all four in action:

  • Second-order: Used hi=2h_i = 2hi​=2 to compute optimal w∗=−G/Hw^* = -G/Hw∗=−G/H
  • Greedy search: Tried one split, computed Gain, accepted it
  • Regularization: η=0.3\eta = 0.3η=0.3 prevented overshoot
  • System: (Implicit) The sorted column block made split finding fast

📘 SECTION 12: SYSTEM DESIGN & ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATIONS

🎯 What This Section Covers

This section explains why XGBoost is not just a better algorithm — it’s a better system. We cover the data structures, parallel strategies, and hardware-aware optimizations that make it scale to millions of samples. By the end, you’ll understand every engineering decision in the original XGBoost paper.

WHY SYSTEM DESIGN MATTERS

🧠 THE CORE PROBLEM

Machine learning algorithms are usually designed as math on paper. Engineers then implement them and hope they run fast.

XGBoost’s insight: Algorithm and system must be co-designed.

Traditional Approach XGBoost Approach
Design algorithm first, optimize later Algorithm designed WITH hardware constraints in mind
Memory access is an afterthought Memory access is the PRIMARY bottleneck
Single-threaded, scan all data Parallel, cache-aware, compressed

From the paper: “The most important factor is the computation pattern of the sparsity aware algorithm… memory access becomes the bottleneck.”

7.1 COLUMN BLOCK STRUCTURE (THE FOUNDATION)

📌 DEFINITION BOX

Column Block = Data stored in Compressed Column (CSC) format where each feature’s values are stored contiguously in sorted order, along with their row indices.

In plain English: Instead of storing data row-by-row (like a CSV), XGBoost stores it column-by-column, sorted, and compressed. This makes finding splits extremely fast.

🔬 WHY COLUMN BLOCKS MATTER

Standard data layout (Row-major):

Row 1: [age=25, income=50000, city=NYC]
Row 2: [age=30, income=60000, city=LA]
Row 3: [age=22, income=45000, city=SF]
...

To find the best split on “age”, you must scan ALL rows. Cache misses everywhere.

XGBoost Column Block (Column-major, sorted):

Feature "age" (sorted):
Value:  [22, 25, 30, 35, 40, ...]
Index:  [3,  1,  2,  5,  4,  ...]  (which row each value came from)

Feature "income" (sorted):
Value:  [30000, 45000, 50000, 60000, ...]
Index:  [10,   3,     1,     2,     ...]

Benefits:

Benefit Explanation
Sorted values To find split points, just scan the sorted list — O(n) instead of O(n log n)
Contiguous memory CPU reads sequential memory = cache-friendly
Reusable Sort once, use for all trees
Parallel features Each feature’s block can be processed by a different CPU core

🧠 DEEP INSIGHT

Column blocks transform the split finding problem from “sort and scan” to “just scan.”

Time complexity per split:

  • Naive: O(n⋅d⋅log⁡n)O(n \cdot d \cdot \log n)O(n⋅d⋅logn) — sort each feature every time
  • XGBoost: O(n⋅d)O(n \cdot d)O(n⋅d) — pre-sorted, just scan

For n=1,000,000n = 1,000,000n=1,000,000 and d=500d = 500d=500, this is the difference between hours and minutes.

7.2 CACHE-AWARE ACCESS PATTERNS

🧠 THE HARDWARE REALITY

Modern CPUs have a memory hierarchy:

Memory Level Size Speed What It Holds
L1 Cache 32-64 KB ~1-2 ns Most frequently used data
L2 Cache 256-512 KB ~3-10 ns Recently used data
L3 Cache 4-32 MB ~10-50 ns Shared across cores
RAM 8-512 GB ~50-100 ns Main memory
Disk/SSD TB ~1-10 ms Persistent storage

Critical fact: A CPU can do billions of operations per second, but waiting for RAM takes 100+ cycles. Memory access, not computation, is the bottleneck.

🔬 THE PROBLEM WITH NAIVE ACCESS

When computing gradients for split finding:

  1. Read feature value from column block → cache miss (data in RAM)
  2. Look up gradient for that sample → cache miss (gradients stored elsewhere)
  3. Accumulate into left/right sum → computation
  4. Repeat for millions of samples

Result: CPU spends 90% of time waiting for memory.

💡 XGBOOST’S SOLUTION: CACHE-AWARE PREFETCHING

Idea: Store gradients and Hessians in a way that matches how the CPU reads them.

Implementation:

  1. Prefetch buffer: Before processing a block of samples, load their gradients into L1 cache
  2. Inner loop access pattern: Access gradients in the exact same order as feature values
  3. Non-blocking: While computing current split, prefetch next batch
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STANDARD ACCESS (Cache misses everywhere)                   │
│                                                              │
│  for each sample:                                            │
│    read feature[i]      → cache miss (go to RAM)            │
│    read gradient[i]     → cache miss (different location)  │
│    accumulate             → wait... wait...                 │
│                                                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  XGBOOST CACHE-AWARE (Prefetch into L1)                      │
│                                                              │
│  prefetch gradients[0:64] into L1 cache                      │
│  for each sample in batch:                                   │
│    read feature[i]      → cache hit (in L1)               │
│    read gradient[i]     → cache hit (prefetched)            │
│    accumulate             → fast!                           │
│  prefetch next batch...                                      │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🧠 INSIGHT

XGBoost makes memory access predictable. The CPU can prefetch data before it’s needed, eliminating wait time.

Result: 2-3x speedup over naive implementations on the same hardware.

7.3 OUT-OF-CORE COMPUTING (DATA LARGER THAN RAM)

🧠 THE PROBLEM

Your dataset is 100 GB. Your computer has 16 GB RAM.

Standard approach: Crash, or use a distributed cluster (expensive).

💡 XGBOOST’S SOLUTION: DISK-BASED LEARNING

Key insight: You don’t need all data in memory at once. You only need the data for the current tree’s split finding.

Algorithm:

Step Action
1 Divide data into blocks that fit in RAM (e.g., 4 GB each)
2 Compress each block (XGBoost uses column-wise compression)
3 For each feature, load one block at a time into RAM
4 Compute partial statistics (G, H) for that block
5 Aggregate across all blocks to get final G, H
6 Discard block, load next one

🔬 BLOCK COMPRESSION

Aspect How It Works
Column-wise Each feature compressed independently
Algorithm Specialized for integer/floating-point data
Decompression On-the-fly during split finding
Tradeoff Slightly more CPU for much less disk I/O

Why column-wise?

  • Features have different ranges (age 0-100, income 0-1,000,000)
  • Different compression strategies per feature
  • Random access to specific features without decompressing everything

🧠 INSIGHT

XGBoost treats disk as “slow RAM” rather than requiring everything in fast RAM.

Result: You can train on datasets 5-10x larger than your RAM on a single machine.

7.4 PARALLEL SPLIT FINDING

🧠 THE OPPORTUNITY

Finding the best split for different features is independent:

  • Feature “age” best split → computed by Core 1
  • Feature “income” best split → computed by Core 2
  • Feature “city” best split → computed by Core 3

These don’t depend on each other!

🔬 TWO LEVELS OF PARALLELISM

Level What Is Parallelized Granularity
Feature-level Different features assigned to different threads Coarse
Node-level Different nodes in the tree processed in parallel Fine

📌 FEATURE-LEVEL PARALLELISM (THE MAIN ONE)

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  THREAD 1 (Core 1)          THREAD 2 (Core 2)              │
│                                                              │
│  Feature: "age"             Feature: "income"               │
│                                                              │
│  Scan sorted values         Scan sorted values               │
│  Compute GL, HL, GR, HR     Compute GL, HL, GR, HR         │
│  Find best split            Find best split                  │
│  Return (Gain, split)       Return (Gain, split)           │
│                                                              │
│                    ↓                                         │
│              MAIN THREAD                                     │
│         Compare all Gains                                   │
│         Pick best feature & split                           │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Why this works:

  • Column blocks are independent
  • No shared state during scan
  • Only synchronize at the end (compare Gains)

⚠️ WHAT IS NOT PARALLELIZED

Operation Why Not Parallel
Tree building across iterations Tree ttt needs residuals from trees 111 to t−1t-1t−1
Split finding within one feature Must scan in order to accumulate GL, HL

Trees are sequential. Features within one tree are parallel.

7.5 APPROXIMATE SPLIT FINDING (WEIGHTED QUANTILE SKETCH)

🧠 THE PROBLEM

For datasets with billions of samples, even scanning sorted column blocks is too slow.

Example: 1 billion rows, 1000 features = 1 trillion values to scan per iteration.

💡 SOLUTION: APPROXIMATE WITH HISTOGRAMS

Idea: Instead of trying every possible split point, group samples into bins and only try split points at bin boundaries.

Approach Split Points Accuracy Speed
Exact Every unique value Perfect Slow
Approximate (histogram) Bin boundaries Good Fast
Very coarse Few bins Lower Very fast

🔬 WEIGHTED QUANTILE SKETCH

Standard quantiles: Divide data into kkk equal-sized groups.

Weighted quantiles: Divide data into kkk groups where each group has roughly equal total Hessian weight.

Why weighted by Hessian?

Reason Explanation
Hessian = confidence Samples with high Hessian are more “important” for the split
Balanced bins Each bin contributes roughly equally to the objective
Theoretical guarantee The approximation error is bounded

📌 ALGORITHM

Step 1: For each feature, compute weighted quantiles

Step 2: Use quantile boundaries as candidate split points

Step 3: Compute Gain only at these boundaries (not all values)

Result: O(number of bins)O(\text{number of bins})O(number of bins) instead of O(number of samples)O(\text{number of samples})O(number of samples)

🧠 INSIGHT

Weighted quantile sketch is not just a hack — it has theoretical guarantees on approximation quality.

From the paper: “We propose a novel distributed weighted quantile sketch algorithm to handle weighted data, which is a common requirement for machine learning tasks.”

7.6 SPARSITY-AWARE SPLIT FINDING

🧠 THE PROBLEM

Real data is sparse:

  • Missing values (NaN)
  • One-hot encoding (mostly zeros)
  • TF-IDF text features (mostly zeros)

Naive approach: Scan all values, including zeros. Wastes 90% of computation.

💡 XGBOOST’S SOLUTION: SKIP ZEROS

Key insight: For many split points, zero values don’t affect the Gain calculation.

Algorithm:

Step Action
1 For each feature, identify non-zero values and their positions
2 When computing GL,HLG_L, H_LGL​,HL​ for a split, only accumulate non-zero entries
3 Missing values are handled by the default direction mechanism (Section 4)

Result: O(non-zeros)O(\text{non-zeros})O(non-zeros) instead of O(all values)O(\text{all values})O(all values)

🧠 INSIGHT

XGBoost treats sparsity as a feature, not a bug. It uses the sparse structure to skip computation, rather than filling in zeros and scanning them.

🔬 COMPLETE SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              XGBOOST SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  DATA LAYER                                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  Column Block Storage (CSC format)                  │   │
│  │  • Sorted per feature                                │   │
│  │  • Compressed (column-wise)                        │   │
│  │  • Sparse-aware (skip zeros)                         │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                              │
│  CACHE LAYER                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  Gradient/Hessian Buffer                             │   │
│  │  • Prefetched into L1/L2 cache                       │   │
│  │  • Access pattern matches computation                 │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                              │
│  COMPUTATION LAYER                                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  Parallel Split Finding                              │   │
│  │  • Feature-level parallelism (multi-core)             │   │
│  │  • Exact or approximate (histogram)                 │   │
│  │                                                      │   │
│  │  Tree Building                                       │   │
│  │  • Greedy, Gain-guided                              │   │
│  │  • Sequential across iterations                     │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                              │
│  MEMORY LAYER (Out-of-Core)                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  Block Streaming                                     │   │
│  │  • Load compressed blocks from disk                  │   │
│  │  • Decompress on-the-fly                            │   │
│  │  • Aggregate statistics, discard block                │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🧠 DEEP INSIGHT: WHY XGBOOST IS 10x–100x FASTER

Innovation Speedup Factor Why
Column blocks (pre-sorted) 3-5x Eliminates sorting per iteration
Cache-aware prefetching 2-3x Reduces memory wait time
Parallel feature scanning 2-8x (cores) Uses all CPU cores
Approximate split (histogram) 10-50x O(bins)O(\text{bins})O(bins) vs O(samples)O(\text{samples})O(samples)
Sparsity-aware (skip zeros) 2-10x Avoids wasted computation
Block compression + out-of-core Enables larger data Train on data > RAM

Combined effect: 10x–100x faster than naive GBM implementations.

⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES

❌ Wrong Idea ✅ Correct Understanding
“XGBoost is fast only because of C++” The speed comes from algorithm-system co-design, not just language
“Parallel means all trees build at once” NO. Trees are sequential. Features within one tree are parallel
“Approximate splits are worse” They have theoretical guarantees and are often indistinguishable in accuracy
“Out-of-core is only for big data” Even medium data benefits from compression (better cache use)
“I don’t need to understand the system” Understanding helps you tune tree_method, max_bin, etc.

🔭 ADVANCED: TUNING SYSTEM PARAMETERS

Parameter What It Controls When to Change
tree_method Algorithm: exact, approx, hist, gpu_hist Use hist or gpu_hist for large data
max_bin Number of bins for histogram More bins = more accurate, slower
subsample Fraction of rows per tree Reduces memory per tree
colsample_bytree Fraction of features per tree Reduces memory, increases diversity

🧾 FINAL NOTEBOOK SUMMARY

Write this in your notes:

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║     XGBOOST SYSTEM DESIGN & ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATIONS        ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                              ║
║  CORE PRINCIPLE: Algorithm + System co-designed              ║
║  Memory access is the bottleneck, not computation           ║
║                                                              ║
║  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ║
║  7.1 COLUMN BLOCK STRUCTURE                                  ║
║  • Store data column-wise, sorted, compressed               ║
║  • CSC format → fast split finding                           ║
║  • Sort once, reuse for all trees                            ║
║  • Enables parallel feature processing                       ║
║                                                              ║
║  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ║
║  7.2 CACHE-AWARE ACCESS                                     ║
║  • Prefetch gradients into L1 cache                          ║
║  • Match access pattern to computation                       ║
║  • Reduces memory wait time                                  ║
║                                                              ║
║  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ║
║  7.3 OUT-OF-CORE COMPUTING                                  ║
║  • Data > RAM? No problem                                    ║
║  • Compress into blocks, stream from disk                    ║
║  • Aggregate statistics per block                            ║
║                                                              ║
║  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ║
║  7.4 PARALLEL SPLIT FINDING                                 ║
║  • Feature-level: different cores scan different features   ║
║  • NOT tree-level: trees are sequential                      ║
║  • Synchronize only at end (compare Gains)                  ║
║                                                              ║
║  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ║
║  7.5 APPROXIMATE SPLIT (WEIGHTED QUANTILE SKETCH)           ║
║  • Group samples into bins (weighted by Hessian)             ║
║  • Only try split at bin boundaries                          ║
║  • O(bins) instead of O(samples)                            ║
║  • Has theoretical approximation guarantees                  ║
║                                                              ║
║  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ║
║  7.6 SPARSITY-AWARE SPLIT FINDING                           ║
║  • Skip zero values entirely                                 ║
║  • Missing values → learned default direction                ║
║  • O(non-zeros) instead of O(all values)                    ║
║                                                              ║
║  ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ║
║  COMBINED SPEEDUP: 10x–100x vs naive GBM                   ║
║                                                              ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

📝 SPACE FOR YOUR HANDWRITTEN NOTES

[Draw the memory hierarchy pyramid: L1 Cache (tiny, fast) at top, then L2, L3, RAM (big, slow), Disk (huge, very slow) at bottom. Draw an arrow showing XGBoost’s strategy: keep hot data in L1, stream cold data from disk. Label “Column Blocks” and “Prefetch Buffer” at the cache levels.]

[Draw the parallel split finding diagram: 4 CPU cores, each scanning a different feature’s column block. Arrows converge to “Compare Gains” and “Pick Best Split”.]

🧪 QUICK SELF-CHECK

Q1: Why does XGBoost store data in column blocks instead of rows?

Answer: Three reasons: (1) Values are pre-sorted, so finding split points is just a scan instead of sort+scan. (2) Memory is contiguous, so CPU cache works efficiently. (3) Different features can be processed in parallel by different CPU cores.

Q2: Your dataset is 200 GB but your server has 32 GB RAM. Can XGBoost handle this?

Answer: Yes, using out-of-core computing. XGBoost compresses data into blocks, loads one block at a time from disk, computes partial statistics, and discards the block. It never needs all 200 GB in RAM simultaneously.

Q3: Why are trees built sequentially but features processed in parallel?

Answer: Tree ttt needs the residuals (gradients/Hessians) from the model after trees 111 to t−1t-1t−1. So trees MUST be sequential. But finding the best split for “age” and “income” are independent calculations — they can run on different cores simultaneously.

Q4: What is the difference between exact split finding and approximate (histogram)?

Answer: Exact tries every unique value as a split point — accurate but slow (O(n)O(n)O(n) per feature). Approximate groups values into bins and only tries bin boundaries — slightly less accurate but much faster (O(bins)O(\text{bins})O(bins) per feature). For large datasets, approximate is often indistinguishable in accuracy.

Q5: Why does the weighted quantile sketch use Hessian as weight instead of just counting samples?

Answer: Because samples with higher Hessian contribute more to the objective function’s curvature. The Hessian represents “confidence” — high-Hessian samples are more informative for split quality. Weighting by Hessian ensures each bin has equal “optimization importance.”

📘 FINAL SECTION: PRACTICAL IMPLEMENTATION, COMPLETE CODE & ALL DIAGRAMS

🎯 What This Section Covers

This final section provides:

  1. Complete Python implementation of XGBoost core concepts from scratch
  2. All mathematical formulas implemented as working code
  3. Visualizations of every key concept
  4. A complete diagram reference for your handwritten notes
  5. End-to-end example with real data

PART A: COMPLETE PYTHON IMPLEMENTATION FROM SCRATCH

A.1 SETUP & UTILITIES

"""
COMPLETE XGBOOST FROM SCRATCH
=============================
This code implements the core XGBoost algorithm
using only NumPy. No external ML libraries.
"""

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from typing import List, Tuple, Dict, Optional
import warnings

# For clean output display
np.set_printoptions(precision=4, suppress=True)

print("=" * 60)
print("XGBOOST FROM SCRATCH - COMPLETE IMPLEMENTATION")
print("=" * 60)

A.2 DATA STRUCTURES: TREE NODE

class TreeNode:
    """
    A node in the XGBoost decision tree.
    
    Can be either:
    - Internal node: has split feature, threshold, left/right children
    - Leaf node: has a weight value w_j*
    """
    
    def __init__(self):
        # For internal nodes
        self.feature_idx: Optional[int] = None      # Which feature to split on
        self.threshold: Optional[float] = None       # Split threshold
        self.left: Optional['TreeNode'] = None      # Left child (x <= threshold)
        self.right: Optional['TreeNode'] = None     # Right child (x > threshold)
        self.default_left: bool = True              # Default direction for missing values
        
        # For leaf nodes
        self.is_leaf: bool = False
        self.weight: float = 0.0                     # w_j* = -G_j / (H_j + lambda)
        
        # For tracking
        self.gain: float = 0.0                       # Gain of the split that created this node
        
    def predict(self, x: np.ndarray) -> float:
        """Predict for a single sample by traversing the tree."""
        if self.is_leaf:
            return self.weight
        
        # Handle missing values (NaN)
        if np.isnan(x[self.feature_idx]):
            if self.default_left:
                return self.left.predict(x)
            else:
                return self.right.predict(x)
        
        # Standard split
        if x[self.feature_idx] <= self.threshold:
            return self.left.predict(x)
        else:
            return self.right.predict(x)
    
    def __repr__(self):
        if self.is_leaf:
            return f"Leaf(weight={self.weight:.4f})"
        return f"Node(feat={self.feature_idx}, thresh={self.threshold:.4f}, gain={self.gain:.4f})"


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("A.2 TREE NODE STRUCTURE DEFINED")
print("=" * 60)
print("""
TreeNode can be:
  - INTERNAL: feature_idx, threshold, left, right, default_left
  - LEAF:     is_leaf=True, weight=w_j*

Missing values route to default_left or default_right based on
which direction gives higher Gain (learned during training).
""")

A.3 LOSS FUNCTIONS: GRADIENT & HESSIAN

class LossFunction:
    """
    Base class for loss functions.
    Each loss must provide gradient (g) and Hessian (h).
    """
    
    def loss(self, y: np.ndarray, y_pred: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def gradient(self, y: np.ndarray, y_pred: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """First derivative: g_i = dl/d(y_pred)"""
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def hessian(self, y: np.ndarray, y_pred: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Second derivative: h_i = d²l/d(y_pred)²"""
        raise NotImplementedError


class MSELoss(LossFunction):
    """
    Mean Squared Error: l(y, y_pred) = (y - y_pred)²
    
    Gradient: g = -2(y - y_pred) = 2(y_pred - y)
    Hessian:  h = 2 (constant!)
    """
    
    def loss(self, y, y_pred):
        return (y - y_pred) ** 2
    
    def gradient(self, y, y_pred):
        # COMPLETE DERIVATION:
        # l = (y - y_pred)²
        # dl/d(y_pred) = 2(y - y_pred) * (-1) = -2(y - y_pred) = 2(y_pred - y)
        return 2 * (y_pred - y)
    
    def hessian(self, y, y_pred):
        # d²l/d(y_pred)² = d/d(y_pred)[2(y_pred - y)] = 2
        return np.full_like(y, 2.0)


class LogLoss(LossFunction):
    """
    Logistic Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)
    
    For classification with labels y ∈ {0, 1}
    
    l(y, z) = y*log(1 + exp(-z)) + (1-y)*log(1 + exp(z))
    
    where z = raw score (log-odds), p = sigmoid(z) = 1/(1 + exp(-z))
    
    Gradient: g = p - y
    Hessian:  h = p * (1 - p)
    """
    
    def _sigmoid(self, z):
        # Numerically stable sigmoid
        return np.where(z >= 0, 
                        1 / (1 + np.exp(-z)), 
                        np.exp(z) / (1 + np.exp(z)))
    
    def loss(self, y, z):
        p = self._sigmoid(z)
        # Numerically stable log loss
        return -y * np.log(p + 1e-15) - (1 - y) * np.log(1 - p + 1e-15)
    
    def gradient(self, y, z):
        # COMPLETE DERIVATION:
        # l = -y*log(p) - (1-y)*log(1-p)
        # p = sigmoid(z) = 1/(1 + exp(-z))
        # dp/dz = p*(1-p)
        # dl/dz = -y*(1/p)*dp/dz + (1-y)*(1/(1-p))*dp/dz
        #       = -y*(1-p) + (1-y)*p
        #       = p - y
        p = self._sigmoid(z)
        return p - y
    
    def hessian(self, y, z):
        # d²l/dz² = d/dz(p - y) = dp/dz = p*(1-p)
        p = self._sigmoid(z)
        return p * (1 - p)


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("A.3 LOSS FUNCTIONS IMPLEMENTED")
print("=" * 60)

# DEMONSTRATION
y_demo = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 0.0])
z_demo = np.array([2.0, -1.0, 0.5, -2.0])

mse = MSELoss()
logloss = LogLoss()

print("\n--- MSE Loss Demo ---")
print(f"y      = {y_demo}")
print(f"z_pred = {z_demo}")
print(f"Loss   = {mse.loss(y_demo, z_demo)}")
print(f"Grad   = {mse.gradient(y_demo, z_demo)}  [g = 2(z - y)]")
print(f"Hess   = {mse.hessian(y_demo, z_demo)}  [h = 2, constant!]")

print("\n--- Log Loss Demo ---")
p_demo = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z_demo))
print(f"y      = {y_demo}")
print(f"z      = {z_demo}")
print(f"p      = {p_demo}  [sigmoid(z)]")
print(f"Loss   = {logloss.loss(y_demo, z_demo)}")
print(f"Grad   = {logloss.gradient(y_demo, z_demo)}  [g = p - y]")
print(f"Hess   = {logloss.hessian(y_demo, z_demo)}  [h = p(1-p)]")

A.4 THE GAIN FORMULA (EXACT IMPLEMENTATION)

def compute_gain(G_L: float, H_L: float, G_R: float, H_R: float, 
                 G_total: float, H_total: float, 
                 lam: float, gamma: float) -> float:
    """
    Compute Gain for a split using the EXACT XGBoost formula.
    
    Gain = 1/2 * [GL²/(HL+λ) + GR²/(HR+λ) - G²/(H+λ)] - γ
    
    Where:
      GL, HL = gradient/Hessian sum in LEFT child
      GR, HR = gradient/Hessian sum in RIGHT child  
      G = GL + GR, H = HL + HR (parent totals)
      λ = L2 regularization
      γ = complexity penalty per leaf
    """
    
    # COMPLETE FORMULA FROM SECTION 4:
    gain = 0.5 * (
        (G_L ** 2) / (H_L + lam) + 
        (G_R ** 2) / (H_R + lam) - 
        (G_total ** 2) / (H_total + lam)
    ) - gamma
    
    return gain


def compute_leaf_weight(G_j: float, H_j: float, lam: float) -> float:
    """
    Compute optimal leaf weight: w_j* = -G_j / (H_j + λ)
    
    From Section 3: derived by setting dL/dw_j = 0
    """
    # DERIVATION:
    # L_j = G_j*w_j + 1/2*(H_j + λ)*w_j²
    # dL/dw_j = G_j + (H_j + λ)*w_j = 0
    # w_j* = -G_j / (H_j + λ)
    
    if H_j + lam == 0:
        return 0.0
    
    return -G_j / (H_j + lam)


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("A.4 GAIN FORMULA & LEAF WEIGHT IMPLEMENTED")
print("=" * 60)

# NUMERICAL EXAMPLE FROM SECTION 4
G_L, H_L = 6, 2
G_R, H_R = 4, 3
G_total, H_total = 10, 5
lam, gamma = 1, 2

gain = compute_gain(G_L, H_L, G_R, H_R, G_total, H_total, lam, gamma)
print(f"\nExample: GL={G_L}, HL={H_L}, GR={G_R}, HR={H_R}")
print(f"         λ={lam}, γ={gamma}")
print(f"Gain = {gain:.4f}")
print(f"Decision: {'ACCEPT' if gain > 0 else 'REJECT'} (Gain {'>' if gain > 0 else '<='} 0)")

# LEAF WEIGHT EXAMPLE
G_j, H_j = 50, 5
w_star = compute_leaf_weight(G_j, H_j, lam=0)
print(f"\nLeaf weight (λ=0):  w* = {w_star:.4f}  [aggressive]")
w_star_reg = compute_leaf_weight(G_j, H_j, lam=10)
print(f"Leaf weight (λ=10): w* = {w_star_reg:.4f}  [conservative]")

A.5 TREE BUILDING (GREEDY ALGORITHM)

class XGBoostTree:
    """
    Single XGBoost decision tree.
    Built using greedy algorithm with Gain-based splits.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, max_depth: int = 3, min_child_weight: float = 1.0,
                 lam: float = 1.0, gamma: float = 0.0):
        self.max_depth = max_depth
        self.min_child_weight = min_child_weight
        self.lam = lam
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.root: Optional[TreeNode] = None
        
    def fit(self, X: np.ndarray, g: np.ndarray, h: np.ndarray) -> 'XGBoostTree':
        """
        Build tree to predict negative gradient (or fit g, h structure).
        
        X: features (n_samples, n_features)
        g: gradients (n_samples,)
        h: Hessians (n_samples,)
        """
        self.root = self._build_tree(X, g, h, depth=0)
        return self
    
    def _build_tree(self, X: np.ndarray, g: np.ndarray, h: np.ndarray, 
                    depth: int) -> TreeNode:
        """Recursively build tree using greedy Gain maximization."""
        
        node = TreeNode()
        
        # Base cases
        if depth >= self.max_depth or len(X) <= 1:
            return self._make_leaf(g, h)
        
        # Try to find best split
        best_gain = 0.0
        best_feature = None
        best_threshold = None
        best_default_left = True
        
        n_features = X.shape[1]
        G_total = np.sum(g)
        H_total = np.sum(h)
        
        # Try each feature
        for feat_idx in range(n_features):
            feature_values = X[:, feat_idx]
            
            # Sort by feature value
            sorted_idx = np.argsort(feature_values)
            sorted_g = g[sorted_idx]
            sorted_h = h[sorted_idx]
            sorted_x = feature_values[sorted_idx]
            
            # Cumulative sums for O(n) split evaluation
            G_cumsum = np.cumsum(sorted_g)
            H_cumsum = np.cumsum(sorted_h)
            
            # Try split at each unique value boundary
            for i in range(len(sorted_x) - 1):
                # Skip if same value (can't split between identical values)
                if sorted_x[i] == sorted_x[i + 1]:
                    continue
                
                # Left: samples 0 to i, Right: samples i+1 to end
                G_L = G_cumsum[i]
                H_L = H_cumsum[i]
                G_R = G_total - G_L
                H_R = H_total - H_L
                
                # Check min_child_weight constraint
                if H_L < self.min_child_weight or H_R < self.min_child_weight:
                    continue
                
                # Compute Gain
                gain = compute_gain(G_L, H_L, G_R, H_R, 
                                   G_total, H_total, 
                                   self.lam, self.gamma)
                
                if gain > best_gain:
                    best_gain = gain
                    best_feature = feat_idx
                    best_threshold = (sorted_x[i] + sorted_x[i + 1]) / 2
        
        # If no good split found, make leaf
        if best_feature is None or best_gain <= 0:
            return self._make_leaf(g, h)
        
        # Create split
        node.feature_idx = best_feature
        node.threshold = best_threshold
        node.gain = best_gain
        
        # Split data
        left_mask = X[:, best_feature] <= best_threshold
        # Handle missing values (NaN) - try both directions, pick best
        nan_mask = np.isnan(X[:, best_feature])
        
        if np.any(nan_mask):
            # Try default_left = True
            mask_left_true = left_mask | nan_mask
            G_left_true = np.sum(g[mask_left_true])
            H_left_true = np.sum(h[mask_left_true])
            G_right_true = np.sum(g[~mask_left_true])
            H_right_true = np.sum(h[~mask_left_true])
            gain_true = compute_gain(G_left_true, H_left_true,
                                    G_right_true, H_right_true,
                                    G_total, H_total, self.lam, self.gamma)
            
            # Try default_left = False  
            mask_left_false = left_mask & ~nan_mask
            G_left_false = np.sum(g[mask_left_false])
            H_left_false = np.sum(h[mask_left_false])
            G_right_false = np.sum(g[~mask_left_false])
            H_right_false = np.sum(h[~mask_left_false])
            gain_false = compute_gain(G_left_false, H_left_false,
                                     G_right_false, H_right_false,
                                     G_total, H_total, self.lam, self.gamma)
            
            if gain_true >= gain_false:
                node.default_left = True
                left_mask = mask_left_true
            else:
                node.default_left = False
                left_mask = mask_left_false
        
        # Recursively build children
        node.left = self._build_tree(X[left_mask], g[left_mask], h[left_mask], depth + 1)
        node.right = self._build_tree(X[~left_mask], g[~left_mask], h[~left_mask], depth + 1)
        
        return node
    
    def _make_leaf(self, g: np.ndarray, h: np.ndarray) -> TreeNode:
        """Create a leaf node with optimal weight."""
        leaf = TreeNode()
        leaf.is_leaf = True
        G_j = np.sum(g)
        H_j = np.sum(h)
        leaf.weight = compute_leaf_weight(G_j, H_j, self.lam)
        return leaf
    
    def predict(self, X: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Predict for all samples."""
        return np.array([self.root.predict(x) for x in X])


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("A.5 XGBOOST TREE BUILDER IMPLEMENTED")
print("=" * 60)
print("""
Algorithm:
  1. For each feature, sort samples by feature value
  2. Compute cumulative G, H for O(n) split evaluation
  3. Try all split points, compute Gain
  4. Pick split with max Gain > 0
  5. Handle missing values by trying both directions
  6. Recurse until max_depth or no good split
  7. Leaf weight = -G_j / (H_j + λ)
""")

A.6 COMPLETE XGBOOST MODEL

class XGBoostFromScratch:
    """
    Complete XGBoost implementation.
    
    Model: F(x) = Σ η * f_k(x)
    """
    
    def __init__(self, n_estimators: int = 100, learning_rate: float = 0.1,
                 max_depth: int = 3, min_child_weight: float = 1.0,
                 lam: float = 1.0, gamma: float = 0.0,
                 subsample: float = 1.0, colsample: float = 1.0,
                 loss: str = 'mse', early_stopping_rounds: Optional[int] = None):
        
        # Core parameters (from Section 7 hyperparameter mapping)
        self.n_estimators = n_estimators          # K = number of trees
        self.learning_rate = learning_rate        # η = shrinkage factor
        self.max_depth = max_depth                # Tree depth limit
        self.min_child_weight = min_child_weight  # H_min minimum Hessian sum
        self.lam = lam                            # λ = L2 regularization
        self.gamma = gamma                        # γ = complexity penalty
        
        # Sampling parameters
        self.subsample = subsample                # Row sampling fraction
        self.colsample = colsample                # Column sampling fraction
        
        # Loss function
        if loss == 'mse':
            self.loss_fn = MSELoss()
        elif loss == 'logloss':
            self.loss_fn = LogLoss()
        else:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown loss: {loss}")
        
        # Early stopping
        self.early_stopping_rounds = early_stopping_rounds
        
        # State
        self.trees: List[XGBoostTree] = []
        self.base_score: float = 0.0
        
    def fit(self, X: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray, 
            X_val: Optional[np.ndarray] = None,
            y_val: Optional[np.ndarray] = None) -> 'XGBoostFromScratch':
        """
        Complete training pipeline (Section 6).
        
        Step 0: Initialize
        Step 1-5: For each iteration, compute g,h, build tree, update
        """
        
        n_samples, n_features = X.shape
        
        # STEP 0: INITIALIZATION
        # For regression: start at mean
        # For classification (logloss): start at 0 (log-odds = 0 → p = 0.5)
        if isinstance(self.loss_fn, MSELoss):
            self.base_score = np.mean(y)
        else:
            self.base_score = 0.0
        
        # Current predictions
        y_pred = np.full(n_samples, self.base_score)
        
        # Validation tracking
        best_val_loss = float('inf')
        rounds_without_improvement = 0
        
        print(f"\n{'='*60}")
        print("TRAINING XGBOOST FROM SCRATCH")
        print(f"{'='*60}")
        print(f"Parameters: K={self.n_estimators}, η={self.learning_rate}")
        print(f"            max_depth={self.max_depth}, λ={self.lam}, γ={self.gamma}")
        print(f"            subsample={self.subsample}, colsample={self.colsample}")
        print(f"Loss: {self.loss_fn.__class__.__name__}")
        print(f"Base score: {self.base_score:.4f}")
        
        # MAIN TRAINING LOOP
        for t in range(self.n_estimators):
            
            # STEP 1: COMPUTE GRADIENTS & HESSIANS
            g = self.loss_fn.gradient(y, y_pred)
            h = self.loss_fn.hessian(y, y_pred)
            
            # STEP 1b: ROW SUBSAMPLING (stochastic boosting)
            if self.subsample < 1.0:
                n_subsample = int(n_samples * self.subsample)
                subsample_idx = np.random.choice(n_samples, n_subsample, replace=False)
                X_sub = X[subsample_idx]
                g_sub = g[subsample_idx]
                h_sub = h[subsample_idx]
            else:
                X_sub, g_sub, h_sub = X, g, h
                subsample_idx = np.arange(n_samples)
            
            # STEP 1c: COLUMN SUBSAMPLING
            if self.colsample < 1.0:
                n_colsample = max(1, int(n_features * self.colsample))
                colsample_idx = np.random.choice(n_features, n_colsample, replace=False)
                X_sub = X_sub[:, colsample_idx]
            else:
                colsample_idx = np.arange(n_features)
            
            # STEP 2: BUILD TREE
            tree = XGBoostTree(
                max_depth=self.max_depth,
                min_child_weight=self.min_child_weight,
                lam=self.lam,
                gamma=self.gamma
            )
            tree.fit(X_sub, -g_sub, h_sub)  # Tree predicts negative gradient
            
            # STEP 3: LEAF WEIGHTS already computed in tree building
            
            # STEP 4: UPDATE PREDICTIONS (with shrinkage)
            # For full dataset (not just subsample)
            tree_predictions = np.array([tree.root.predict(x) for x in X])
            y_pred += self.learning_rate * tree_predictions
            
            self.trees.append(tree)
            
            # Compute losses
            train_loss = np.mean(self.loss_fn.loss(y, y_pred))
            
            # STEP 5: EARLY STOPPING
            if X_val is not None and y_val is not None:
                val_pred = self.predict(X_val)
                val_loss = np.mean(self.loss_fn.loss(y_val, val_pred))
                
                if val_loss < best_val_loss:
                    best_val_loss = val_loss
                    rounds_without_improvement = 0
                else:
                    rounds_without_improvement += 1
                
                if (self.early_stopping_rounds and 
                    rounds_without_improvement >= self.early_stopping_rounds):
                    print(f"\nEarly stopping at iteration {t+1}")
                    print(f"Best val loss: {best_val_loss:.6f}")
                    break
                
                if (t + 1) % 10 == 0 or t == 0:
                    print(f"Iter {t+1:3d}: Train Loss={train_loss:.6f}, "
                          f"Val Loss={val_loss:.6f}")
            else:
                if (t + 1) % 10 == 0 or t == 0:
                    print(f"Iter {t+1:3d}: Train Loss={train_loss:.6f}")
        
        print(f"\nTraining complete. Built {len(self.trees)} trees.")
        return self
    
    def predict(self, X: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Make predictions: F(x) = base + Σ η * f_k(x)"""
        y_pred = np.full(len(X), self.base_score)
        
        for tree in self.trees:
            tree_preds = np.array([tree.root.predict(x) for x in X])
            y_pred += self.learning_rate * tree_preds
        
        return y_pred
    
    def predict_proba(self, X: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """For classification: convert log-odds to probability."""
        z = self.predict(X)
        return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("A.6 COMPLETE XGBOOST MODEL IMPLEMENTED")
print("=" * 60)
print("""
Full Pipeline (from Section 6):
  Step 0: Initialize ŷ = base_score
  For t = 1 to K:
    1. Compute g_i, h_i for all samples
    2. Subsample rows (if subsample < 1)
    3. Subsample columns (if colsample < 1)  
    4. Build tree using Gain formula
    5. Compute leaf weights w_j* = -G_j/(H_j+λ)
    6. Update: ŷ ← ŷ + η * f_t(x)
    7. Check early stopping
""")

A.7 END-TO-END DEMO: REGRESSION

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("A.7 END-TO-END DEMO: REGRESSION")
print("=" * 60)

# Generate synthetic regression data
np.random.seed(42)
n_train, n_test = 500, 100

# True function: y = 3*x1 + 2*x2 - 5*x3 + noise
X_train = np.random.randn(n_train, 3)
true_weights = np.array([3.0, 2.0, -5.0])
y_train = X_train @ true_weights + np.random.randn(n_train) * 0.5

X_test = np.random.randn(n_test, 3)
y_test = X_test @ true_weights + np.random.randn(n_test) * 0.5

print(f"\nData: {n_train} train, {n_test} test samples")
print(f"True weights: {true_weights}")

# Train XGBoost from scratch
model = XGBoostFromScratch(
    n_estimators=50,
    learning_rate=0.1,
    max_depth=2,
    lam=1.0,
    gamma=0.0,
    loss='mse',
    early_stopping_rounds=5
)

model.fit(X_train, y_train, X_test, y_test)

# Evaluate
y_pred_train = model.predict(X_train)
y_pred_test = model.predict(X_test)

train_mse = np.mean((y_train - y_pred_train) ** 2)
test_mse = np.mean((y_test - y_pred_test) ** 2)

print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print("RESULTS")
print(f"{'='*60}")
print(f"Train MSE: {train_mse:.4f}")
print(f"Test MSE:  {test_mse:.4f}")
print(f"Number of trees used: {len(model.trees)}")

A.8 END-TO-END DEMO: CLASSIFICATION

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("A.8 END-TO-END DEMO: CLASSIFICATION")
print("=" * 60)

# Generate synthetic classification data
np.random.seed(42)

# Two classes separated by a non-linear boundary
n_per_class = 300
X_class0 = np.random.randn(n_per_class, 2) + np.array([2, 2])
X_class1 = np.random.randn(n_per_class, 2) + np.array([-2, -2])
X_train_cls = np.vstack([X_class0, X_class1])
y_train_cls = np.array([0]*n_per_class + [1]*n_per_class)

# Shuffle
shuffle_idx = np.random.permutation(len(y_train_cls))
X_train_cls = X_train_cls[shuffle_idx]
y_train_cls = y_train_cls[shuffle_idx]

# Test data
X_test_cls = np.random.randn(100, 2)
y_test_cls = (X_test_cls[:, 0] + X_test_cls[:, 1] > 0).astype(int)

print(f"\nData: {len(y_train_cls)} train, {len(y_test_cls)} test samples")
print(f"Class balance: {np.mean(y_train_cls):.2f}")

# Train
model_cls = XGBoostFromScratch(
    n_estimators=30,
    learning_rate=0.3,
    max_depth=2,
    lam=1.0,
    gamma=0.0,
    loss='logloss',
    early_stopping_rounds=5
)

model_cls.fit(X_train_cls, y_train_cls, X_test_cls, y_test_cls)

# Evaluate
y_pred_proba = model_cls.predict_proba(X_test_cls)
y_pred_class = (y_pred_proba > 0.5).astype(int)

accuracy = np.mean(y_pred_class == y_test_cls)
print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print("CLASSIFICATION RESULTS")
print(f"{'='*60}")
print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}")
print(f"Number of trees used: {len(model_cls.trees)}")

# Show some predictions
print(f"\nSample predictions (first 10):")
print(f"True:     {y_test_cls[:10]}")
print(f"Predicted: {y_pred_class[:10]}")
print(f"Prob:     {y_pred_proba[:10].round(4)}")

PART B: VISUALIZATIONS & DIAGRAMS

B.1 VISUALIZATION: TREE STRUCTURE

def visualize_tree(node, depth=0, prefix="Root"):
    """Print tree structure for visualization."""
    indent = "  " * depth
    if node.is_leaf:
        print(f"{indent}{prefix}: Leaf(w={node.weight:.4f})")
    else:
        print(f"{indent}{prefix}: Split feat={node.feature_idx}, "
              f"thresh={node.threshold:.4f}, gain={node.gain:.4f}, "
              f"default_left={node.default_left}")
        visualize_tree(node.left, depth + 1, "Left")
        visualize_tree(node.right, depth + 1, "Right")


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("B.1 TREE STRUCTURE VISUALIZATION")
print("=" * 60)
print("\nFirst tree from regression model:")
visualize_tree(model.trees[0].root)

B.2 VISUALIZATION: TRAINING CONVERGENCE

def plot_convergence(model, X_train, y_train, X_test, y_test, loss_fn):
    """Plot how training and validation loss converge."""
    
    train_losses = []
    val_losses = []
    
    # Simulate training trajectory
    y_pred = np.full(len(X_train), model.base_score)
    
    for tree in model.trees:
        tree_preds = np.array([tree.root.predict(x) for x in X_train])
        y_pred += model.learning_rate * tree_preds
        train_losses.append(np.mean(loss_fn.loss(y_train, y_pred)))
    
    # Validation
    y_pred_val = np.full(len(X_test), model.base_score)
    for tree in model.trees:
        tree_preds = np.array([tree.root.predict(x) for x in X_test])
        y_pred_val += model.learning_rate * tree_preds
        val_losses.append(np.mean(loss_fn.loss(y_test, y_pred_val)))
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    plt.plot(train_losses, 'b-', label='Training Loss', linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(val_losses, 'r-', label='Validation Loss', linewidth=2)
    plt.xlabel('Iteration (Number of Trees)', fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel('Loss', fontsize=12)
    plt.title('XGBoost Training Convergence', fontsize=14)
    plt.legend(fontsize=11)
    plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    
    # Save for notes
    plt.savefig('/mnt/agents/output/xgboost_convergence.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.show()
    
    print("\nConvergence plot saved!")


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("B.2 CONVERGENCE PLOT")
print("=" * 60)
plot_convergence(model, X_train, y_train, X_test, y_test, MSELoss())

B.3 VISUALIZATION: FEATURE IMPORTANCE (GAIN)

def compute_feature_importance(trees, learning_rate, n_features):
    """
    Compute feature importance using Gain (Section 9).
    
    Importance_j = sum of Gain for all splits using feature j
    """
    importance = np.zeros(n_features)
    
    def traverse(node):
        if node.is_leaf:
            return
        if node.feature_idx is not None:
            importance[node.feature_idx] += node.gain
        traverse(node.left)
        traverse(node.right)
    
    for tree in trees:
        traverse(tree.root)
    
    return importance


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("B.3 FEATURE IMPORTANCE (GAIN)")
print("=" * 60)

importance = compute_feature_importance(model.trees, model.learning_rate, 3)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
features = ['Feature 0 (true w=3)', 'Feature 1 (true w=2)', 'Feature 2 (true w=-5)']
colors = ['green' if imp == max(importance) else 'steelblue' for imp in importance]

bars = plt.bar(features, importance, color=colors, edgecolor='black')
plt.ylabel('Total Gain', fontsize=12)
plt.title('XGBoost Feature Importance (Gain)', fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(rotation=15, ha='right')

# Add value labels on bars
for bar, imp in zip(bars, importance):
    plt.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.01,
             f'{imp:.2f}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/mnt/agents/output/xgboost_feature_importance.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

print(f"\nFeature importance (Gain):")
for i, (feat, imp) in enumerate(zip(features, importance)):
    print(f"  {feat}: {imp:.4f}")
print(f"\nMost important: Feature {np.argmax(importance)} "
      f"(matches true weight magnitude: {np.abs(true_weights).argmax()})")

B.4 VISUALIZATION: LEAF WEIGHT DISTRIBUTION

def get_all_leaf_weights(trees):
    """Extract all leaf weights from all trees."""
    weights = []
    
    def traverse(node):
        if node.is_leaf:
            weights.append(node.weight)
        else:
            traverse(node.left)
            traverse(node.right)
    
    for tree in trees:
        traverse(tree.root)
    
    return np.array(weights)


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("B.4 LEAF WEIGHT DISTRIBUTION")
print("=" * 60)

all_weights = get_all_leaf_weights(model.trees)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))

plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.hist(all_weights, bins=30, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.7)
plt.xlabel('Leaf Weight (w_j*)', fontsize=11)
plt.ylabel('Count', fontsize=11)
plt.title('Distribution of Leaf Weights', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.plot(sorted(np.abs(all_weights)), 'o-', color='coral', markersize=3)
plt.xlabel('Leaf Index (sorted by |weight|)', fontsize=11)
plt.ylabel('|Leaf Weight|', fontsize=11)
plt.title('Leaf Weight Magnitudes', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.yscale('log')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/mnt/agents/output/xgboost_leaf_weights.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

print(f"\nLeaf weight statistics:")
print(f"  Count: {len(all_weights)}")
print(f"  Mean: {np.mean(all_weights):.4f}")
print(f"  Std:  {np.std(all_weights):.4f}")
print(f"  Min:  {np.min(all_weights):.4f}")
print(f"  Max:  {np.max(all_weights):.4f}")
print(f"\nRegularization (λ={model.lam}) keeps weights controlled.")

B.5 VISUALIZATION: DECISION BOUNDARY (CLASSIFICATION)

def plot_decision_boundary(model, X, y, title="XGBoost Decision Boundary"):
    """Plot 2D decision boundary for classification."""
    
    # Create mesh
    h = 0.02
    x_min, x_max = X[:, 0].min() - 1, X[:, 0].max() + 1
    y_min, y_max = X[:, 1].min() - 1, X[:, 1].max() + 1
    xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.arange(x_min, x_max, h),
                         np.arange(y_min, y_max, h))
    
    # Predict on mesh
    mesh_points = np.c_[xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()]
    Z = model.predict_proba(mesh_points)
    Z = Z.reshape(xx.shape)
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
    
    # Plot decision boundary
    plt.contourf(xx, yy, Z, levels=50, cmap='RdBu', alpha=0.6)
    plt.colorbar(label='P(Class=1)')
    
    # Plot data points
    scatter = plt.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], c=y, cmap='RdBu', 
                         edgecolors='black', s=50, zorder=5)
    plt.legend(*scatter.legend_elements(), title="Class")
    
    # Plot decision threshold
    plt.contour(xx, yy, Z, levels=[0.5], colors='black', linewidths=2)
    
    plt.xlabel('Feature 1', fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel('Feature 2', fontsize=12)
    plt.title(title, fontsize=14)
    plt.xlim(x_min, x_max)
    plt.ylim(y_min, y_max)
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig('/mnt/agents/output/xgboost_decision_boundary.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.show()


print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("B.5 DECISION BOUNDARY VISUALIZATION")
print("=" * 60)
plot_decision_boundary(model_cls, X_train_cls, y_train_cls,
                      "XGBoost Classification Decision Boundary")

PART C: COMPLETE DIAGRAM REFERENCE FOR HANDWRITTEN NOTES

C.1 THE BIG PICTURE: XGBOOST ARCHITECTURE

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                    XGBOOST COMPLETE ARCHITECTURE                       ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                      ║
║  INPUT: Features x = [x₁, x₂, ..., x_d]                            ║
║         ↓                                                            ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
║  │           ADDITIVE MODEL: F(x) = Σₖ η·fₖ(x)                  │   ║
║  │                                                              │   ║
║  │   Tree 1    Tree 2    Tree 3         Tree K                 │   ║
║  │   ┌───┐    ┌───┐    ┌───┐           ┌───┐                  │   ║
║  │   │+2 │    │-1 │    │+3 │    ...    │+1 │                  │   ║
║  │   └───┘    └───┘    └───┘           └───┘                  │   ║
║  │     ↓        ↓        ↓               ↓                     │   ║
║  │   η·2      η·(-1)    η·3           η·1                      │   ║
║  │     └────────┴────────┴──────┬──────┘                      │   ║
║  │                                ↓                            │   ║
║  │                         Final Prediction                    │   ║
║  │                         ŷ = base + Ση·fₖ(x)              │   ║
║  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
║                                                                      ║
║  For Classification:                                                 ║
║    z = ŷ (raw score/log-odds)                                       ║
║    p = 1/(1 + e^(-z))  ← sigmoid                                    ║
║                                                                      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

C.2 SINGLE TREE STRUCTURE

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║              XGBOOST DECISION TREE STRUCTURE                           ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                      ║
║                         ┌─────────────┐                              ║
║                         │   ROOT      │                              ║
║                         │  x₃ ≤ 2.5?  │ ← Split feature & threshold  ║
║                         │  Gain=15.3  │ ← Quality of this split      ║
║                         └──────┬──────┘                              ║
║                                │                                     ║
║              ┌──────────────────┴──────────────────┐                  ║
║              │ Yes (Left)                        │ No (Right)       ║
║              ↓                                    ↓                  ║
║        ┌─────────┐                         ┌─────────┐             ║
║        │ x₁ ≤ 1? │                         │ x₂ ≤ 5? │             ║
║        │ Gain=8  │                         │ Gain=6  │             ║
║        └────┬────┘                         └────┬────┘             ║
║             │                                     │                  ║
║       ┌─────┴─────┐                       ┌─────┴─────┐            ║
║       ↓           ↓                         ↓           ↓            ║
║    ┌─────┐    ┌─────┐                  ┌─────┐    ┌─────┐         ║
║    │LEAF │    │LEAF │                  │LEAF │    │LEAF │         ║
║    │w=+2 │    │w=-1 │                  │w=+3 │    │w=-2 │         ║
║    │G=20 │    │G=-10│                  │G=30 │    │G=-15│         ║
║    │H=5  │    │H=3  │                  │H=8  │    │H=4  │         ║
║    └─────┘    └─────┘                  └─────┘    └─────┘         ║
║                                                                      ║
║  LEAF NODE:                                                          ║
║    • is_leaf = True                                                  ║
║    • weight = w_j* = -G_j/(H_j + λ)                                  ║
║    • No children                                                     ║
║                                                                      ║
║  INTERNAL NODE:                                                      ║
║    • feature_idx = which feature to test                             ║
║    • threshold = split value                                          ║
║    • default_left = direction for missing values                     ║
║    • left, right = children                                          ║
║                                                                      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

C.3 THE GAIN FORMULA (VISUAL)

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                    SPLIT GAIN FORMULA                                ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                      ║
║  PARENT NODE (before split):                                         ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────┐                                         ║
║  │      G = G_L + G_R      │  ← Total gradient                      ║
║  │      H = H_L + H_R      │  ← Total Hessian                       ║
║  │   Score = G²/(H+λ)      │  ← Parent quality                      ║
║  └─────────────────────────┘                                         ║
║              │                                                       ║
║              │ Split on feature j at threshold t                    ║
║              ↓                                                       ║
║     ┌──────┴──────┐                                                ║
║     ↓             ↓                                                  ║
║  ┌───────┐   ┌───────┐                                             ║
║  │ LEFT  │   │ RIGHT │                                             ║
║  │ G_L   │   │ G_R   │                                             ║
║  │ H_L   │   │ H_R   │                                             ║
║  │Score_L│   │Score_R│                                             ║
║  │=G_L²/ │   │=G_R²/ │                                             ║
║  │(H_L+λ)│   │(H_R+λ)│                                             ║
║  └───────┘   └───────┘                                             ║
║                                                                      ║
║  GAIN = ½[Score_L + Score_R - Score_parent] - γ                    ║
║                                                                      ║
║       = ½[GL²/(HL+λ) + GR²/(HR+λ) - G²/(H+λ)] - γ                  ║
║                                                                      ║
║  DECISION:                                                           ║
║    • Gain > 0  →  ACCEPT split (improvement > complexity cost)       ║
║    • Gain ≤ 0  →  REJECT split (become leaf)                         ║
║                                                                      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

C.4 REGULARIZATION EFFECTS

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║              REGULARIZATION PARAMETER EFFECTS                          ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                      ║
║  λ (L2) EFFECT ON LEAF WEIGHT:                                      ║
║                                                                      ║
║  w_j* = -G_j / (H_j + λ)                                            ║
║                                                                      ║
║  λ=0:   w* = -50/5 = -10.0    ← Aggressive, full correction         ║
║  λ=1:   w* = -50/6 = -8.33    ← Slightly conservative               ║
║  λ=10:  w* = -50/15 = -3.33   ← Very conservative                   ║
║  λ=100: w* = -50/105 = -0.48  ← Almost no learning                  ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  ║
║                                                                      ║
║  γ (GAMMA) EFFECT ON TREE DEPTH:                                     ║
║                                                                      ║
║  γ=0:   Any split accepted    → Deep, complex trees                 ║
║  γ=1:   Good splits only      → Moderate trees                       ║
║  γ=10:  Excellent splits only → Shallow trees                        ║
║  γ=∞:   No splits allowed     → Single leaf (constant model)        ║
║                                                                      ║
║  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  ║
║                                                                      ║
║  η (LEARNING RATE) EFFECT:                                          ║
║                                                                      ║
║  η=1.0: Full correction       → Fast, unstable, overfits           ║
║  η=0.3: Moderate correction   → Balanced                            ║
║  η=0.1: Small correction      → Slow, stable, generalizes well       ║
║  η=0.01: Tiny correction      → Very slow, needs many trees          ║
║                                                                      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

C.5 TRAINING PIPELINE FLOWCHART

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║              COMPLETE TRAINING PIPELINE                              ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                      ║
║   START                                                              ║
║     │                                                                ║
║     ↓                                                                ║
║  ┌─────────────┐                                                     ║
║  │ Initialize  │  ŷ⁽⁰⁾ = 0 (or mean)                               ║
║  └──────┬──────┘                                                     ║
║         │                                                            ║
║         ↓                                                            ║
║  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
